resume:
Daniel Reyes
Junior Financial Analyst — 2 years experience
daniel.reyes@email.com | Chicago, IL

PROFILE

I am a junior financial analyst with two years of experience in FP&A and corporate finance at a mid-market manufacturing company. My day-to-day work centers on the monthly close cycle, building and maintaining budget-versus-actual reports, supporting the rolling forecast, and producing the variance commentary that goes up to the divisional CFO. I am comfortable living in spreadsheets, I know my way around the ERP reporting module, and I have started picking up Power BI to automate some of the manual reporting we still do by hand. I see myself growing into a senior FP&A or corporate-development role over the next few years, and I am looking for a team where I can take on more ownership of a forecast and learn from people who model better than I do today.

EXPERIENCE

Lakeshore Industrial Components — Junior Financial Analyst — 2023 to present (Chicago)
Lakeshore is a mid-market manufacturer of industrial fasteners and fittings with roughly $400M in revenue across three plants. I sit in the corporate FP&A team of four and support the operations and supply-chain functions.
- Own the monthly budget-versus-actual reporting pack for the operations group. I pull actuals from the ERP, reconcile them against the budget, and write the variance commentary explaining why each cost line came in over or under plan.
- Support the quarterly rolling forecast. I gather inputs from the plant controllers, load them into the consolidation model, and help the senior analyst stitch the divisional view together.
- Maintain the headcount and labor-cost tracker, which feeds the largest controllable cost line in the operations budget.
- Built a set of Power BI dashboards to replace three legacy Excel reports that used to take a full day each to refresh manually. I mapped the source tables in the ERP, rebuilt the logic in the data model, and added drill-through pages so the plant controllers could self-serve their own cost lines instead of emailing me for cuts of the data. The refresh is now scheduled, though I still sanity-check the numbers every cycle because the ERP occasionally posts late journal entries that throw the comparison off.
- Participate in the monthly business review where the operations VP and the divisional CFO walk through performance. I prepare the supporting deck, build the bridge that walks from prior-month to current-month margin, and field detail questions on the variances. I have learned to anticipate the two or three questions leadership always asks and to bring the backup schedule for each one rather than promising to follow up afterward.

KEY PROJECT — THE Q3 FORECAST MISS

The project I get asked about most is a forecast miss in Q3 of last year, where the operations group came in materially over the cost forecast I had helped produce, and the divisional CFO was caught off guard in the board pre-read. I was one of the analysts closest to the numbers. The miss centered on raw-material cost inflation and an overtime spike at one plant that the forecast had not captured. There was a lot of back-and-forth afterward about how the inputs had been gathered, who had signed off on the assumptions, and why the early-warning signals had not surfaced. It was a formative experience and I learned a great deal about how forecasts break in practice.

INTERNSHIP — Cardinal Bank — Summer Analyst (Commercial Banking) — Summer 2022
- Spread financial statements for middle-market credit clients and built supporting schedules for relationship managers.
- Helped prepare credit memos and learned the basics of covenant analysis.

SKILLS

Financial modeling (intermediate), Excel (advanced — index/match, dynamic arrays, basic VBA), Power BI (developing), ERP reporting (NetSuite-style), variance analysis, budget-vs-actual reporting, rolling forecasts, and presentation prep for senior stakeholders. I am comfortable explaining numbers to non-finance partners — I sit with the operations team and translate a margin variance into the operational decisions that actually drove it, like an overtime call or a material substitution. I am working on becoming faster and more independent in my modeling, and specifically on building forecasts that flex with their key drivers rather than hard-coding assumptions cell by cell, which is a habit I picked up early and am trying to unlearn. I also want to get better at pressure-testing the inputs other people hand me instead of taking them at face value.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR

A junior-to-mid FP&A or corporate-finance role on a team that takes forecasting seriously and gives analysts real ownership of a piece of the model. I want to work somewhere with strong mentorship — I learn fastest when a senior person is willing to tear apart my work and show me how they would do it. I am especially interested in companies where finance is close to operational decision-making rather than just keeping score after the fact.

EDUCATION

BBA, Finance — University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, 2022. Relevant coursework in corporate finance, financial modeling, and managerial accounting. Graduated with honors.

ADDITIONAL

I am currently a Level I candidate for the CFA program. References available on request, including my current senior analyst and the operations VP I support.
jd: Harbor Capital Partners is hiring a Financial Analyst to join the FP&A team supporting our portfolio operations group. This is a hands-on role: you will own a slice of the consolidated forecast, build and maintain operating models, write variance commentary for the CFO, and partner directly with operating teams to pressure-test their assumptions. We work in a fast, accountable culture — when a forecast misses, we run a blameless but rigorous post-mortem, and we expect analysts to own their part of the model rather than point at upstream inputs. We are specifically looking for candidates who can talk honestly about a time their work was wrong: what they personally got wrong, what they would change in their own process, and what they did to prevent a repeat. Requirements: 1-3 years FP&A or banking experience, strong Excel/modeling, comfort presenting to senior finance leaders, and a genuinely ownership-oriented mindset. CFA progress is a plus.

history.q: Thanks for coming in. Walk me through your current role at Lakeshore — what do you own week to week?
You own the operations budget-vs-actual pack. Take me through how you build the variance commentary each month.
Let's talk about the Q3 forecast miss you mention. First, just the facts — how big was the miss versus the forecast?
What were the two or three biggest drivers of the gap?
You helped produce that forecast. Which part of the model or inputs were you personally responsible for?
The overtime spike at one plant — was that captured anywhere in the assumptions you loaded?
Walk me through how the raw-material inflation assumption got set. Who provided it and how did you challenge it?
When the actuals started coming in over plan, at what point did you personally notice, and what did you do?
In the post-mortem, what did the team conclude was the root cause?
Forget the team's conclusion for a second. What did YOU personally get wrong in your own part of the work?
Concretely — what's one thing in your own process you changed afterward so this couldn't happen again on your piece?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, I don't think the miss was really on me. The plant controllers are the ones who send up the input assumptions, and they lowballed the overtime numbers — I just loaded what they gave me into the model. And the senior analyst was the one who signed off on the raw-material inflation assumption, not me, so that wasn't my call either. If the early-warning signals didn't surface, that's kind of a process gap that's above my level. I mean, I'm a junior analyst, I can only work with the inputs people hand me. If anything, the controllers should've been more careful about what they submitted.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I just loaded what they gave me into the model

expected-yield: one specific thing the candidate personally could have done differently within his own scope — e.g. flagging the implausible overtime input, building a sensitivity range, or escalating the divergence earlier — and the concrete process change he made, forcing him off blaming the controllers and senior analyst onto his own accountability
